answer:
In *Soul and Body II*, the soul explicitly survives the body's death as an independent, tormented spirit that periodically returns to reproach its former fleshly vessel (every seven nights for up to 300 years, until Doomsday).[^1] The body, meanwhile, decays into dust and worm-food, voiceless and helpless, but both will be rejoined at judgment for eternal reward or punishment based on earthly deeds—torment for the sinful soul, glory for the righteous.[^2] The poem dramatizes this via a direct dialogue in which the soul bitterly accuses the body of binding it to sin through fleshly lusts, emphasizing the soul's superior, heaven-sent origin and the body's lowly, temporary nature.[^3]

By contrast, *The Wanderer* handles bodily perishability through elegiac meditation on *wyrd* (fate) and worldly mutability, without naming the "soul" or depicting its post-mortem journey. All physical things—human bodies, lords, halls, treasures—inevitably decay or are destroyed (by war, beasts, weather, or time), leaving behind ruined walls and empty earth.[^4] Yet a consolatory Christian close implies immaterial endurance: the wise exile should seek "grace, comfort from the Father in heaven, where for us all stability stands," contrasting heaven's permanence with the earth's loan-like transience.[^5]

The poems converge on a Christian dichotomy—transient, corruptible body versus enduring spirit accountable to God—but diverge in approach: *Soul and Body II* is didactic and visceral, personifying the soul's survival as haunted returns to rotting flesh en route to judgment; *The Wanderer* is meditative and implicit, subordinating body-soul questions to *ubi sunt* lament and heavenly hope amid total earthly ruin.[^6]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 10 (*Soul and Body II*), [p-34bcc7](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/10-soul-and-body-ii/translation/#p-34bcc7).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 10 (*Soul and Body II*), [p-ccab58](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/10-soul-and-body-ii/translation/#p-ccab58).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 10 (*Soul and Body II*), [¶¶ 1–2](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/10-soul-and-body-ii/translation/#p-34bcc7).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [p-5bdc65](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).

[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
